title: 3-year-old with expressive language delay — Medicaid speech-therapy prior-auth denial

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: Thanks for coming in. I understand you have concerns about your son's talking. Tell me what you've been noticing at home.

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: He turned three last month, and he's barely talking. He has maybe twenty words he actually uses, and half of those only we can understand.

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: Is he putting words together at all — two at a time, like 'more juice' or 'daddy go'?

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: Never. It's one word at a time. 'Juice.' 'Up.' Mostly he points and grunts, and if we don't figure it out fast enough he melts down.

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: How often do those meltdowns happen?

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: Every day, sometimes three or four times. Yesterday he threw himself on the floor because he wanted a specific cup and couldn't tell me which one.

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: That's very common when a child can't get his message across. Does he understand what you say to him? If you ask him to go get his shoes, does he do it?

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: He understands everything. He follows directions fine. It's getting the words out that's the problem.

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: That tells me comprehension is a relative strength. Any concerns about his hearing?

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: We had it checked. The hearing screen at the pediatrician's office was normal — he passed in both ears.

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: Good. A normal hearing screen matters — it means we're not missing a hearing loss underneath this. Was he getting any services before now?

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: He was in Early Intervention. A therapist came to the house, but that stopped when he turned three. They said he aged out and now it goes through insurance or the school district.

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: Right — Early Intervention ends at the third birthday. Did they leave you with things to work on at home?

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: Yes, and we do them every day. We narrate everything, we offer choices, we hold things up and wait for him to try the word, we read every night. We've done it for almost a year and he's gained only a handful of words.

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: So a parent-implemented program has been in place consistently, and the gap hasn't closed. That matters for what I'm going to recommend. Today I gave him a standardized test, the Preschool Language Scales, Fifth Edition. On the expressive side — the words he produces — he scored below the fifth percentile for his age. His understanding scored in the average range.

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: Below the fifth percentile. So out of a hundred kids his age, more than ninety-five are talking better than he is?

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: That's a fair way to read it. It confirms what you're describing: a significant expressive language delay with intact comprehension and normal hearing.

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: People keep telling me boys talk late and he'll catch up on his own.

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: Some late talkers do catch up. But at three years old, with about twenty words, no two-word combinations, and this much frustration, he's outside the range where waiting is the right plan — especially when a year of home support hasn't closed the gap.

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: Day to day he can't even ask for water. He'll stand at the refrigerator and scream.

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: That's the functional piece: he can't request basic needs, he can't use words to protest, and the frustration comes out as tantrums. It affects meals, preschool readiness, safety — all of it.

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: So what do you recommend?

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: Skilled speech-language therapy twice a week, working on building his vocabulary and getting two-word combinations started. I'll write measurable goals — for example, twenty-five spontaneous single words across settings, then ten different two-word combinations — and I'll re-test him in about twelve weeks to see whether the plan is working.

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: Can't we just keep doing the home program instead?

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: Please keep doing it — it helps, and I'll coach you further in every session. But at this level of delay he needs direct treatment from a licensed therapist. The home program alone hasn't been enough, and that is not your fault.

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: What happens with insurance?

encounter_transcript.speaker: slp
encounter_transcript.text: I'll send the plan of care and the test results to the plan for prior authorization. If they deny it, you have appeal rights, and because he's on Medicaid there are added protections for children — the standard for kids is whether treatment corrects or improves the problem, not whether it restores something he lost.

encounter_transcript.speaker: parent
encounter_transcript.text: Okay. I just want him to be able to tell me what he needs.

clinical_note:
CHIEF CONCERN: Expressive language delay in a 3-year-old established patient.

HISTORY: The patient is a 3-year-old boy referred for speech-language evaluation because of limited spoken language. His parent reports an expressive vocabulary of approximately 20 words, none of which are combined into two-word phrases. Receptive language is a relative strength: he follows two-step directions at home without gesture cues. Communication breakdowns produce daily frustration behaviors, including tantrums and dropping to the floor, most often when he cannot make a request understood. He received Early Intervention services for expressive language delay until aging out of that program at his third birthday. His parent has carried out a parent-implemented language stimulation program (environmental narration, forced-choice questions, expectant waiting, nightly shared book reading) consistently for approximately one year, with only minimal vocabulary gain over that period.

STANDARDIZED ASSESSMENT: The Preschool Language Scales, Fifth Edition (PLS-5) was administered during this visit by the evaluating speech-language pathologist. The Expressive Communication score fell below the 5th percentile for age. The Auditory Comprehension score was within the average range. Test behavior was cooperative, and results are judged a valid estimate of current functioning.

HEARING: The patient passed a pediatric hearing screen in both ears; hearing is normal. Hearing loss is excluded as the primary cause of the expressive delay.

FUNCTIONAL DEFICITS: The patient cannot verbally request basic needs such as food, drink, or help; he cannot protest or comment with words and relies on pointing, pulling, and vocalization. Failed communication attempts end in tantrums several times per day and are interfering with mealtimes, toileting routines, preschool readiness, and household safety.

PLAN: Skilled speech-language therapy 2 times per week (individual 30-minute sessions) targeting expressive vocabulary expansion and emergence of two-word combinations, delivered by a licensed speech-language pathologist with caregiver coaching embedded in each session. Measurable goals: (1) spontaneous use of at least 25 different single words across at least two settings; (2) production of at least 10 different two-word combinations with no more than one verbal model. The home language program will continue between sessions. Formal re-evaluation of expressive language with a standardized measure is scheduled in 12 weeks to measure progress against these goals and adjust the plan of care.
denial_letter:
NOTICE OF ADVERSE BENEFIT DETERMINATION

Re: Prior authorization request — outpatient speech-language therapy, two sessions per week

Dear Parent or Guardian,

We have completed our review of the prior authorization request submitted by your child's provider for outpatient speech-language therapy services. After careful review by our clinical staff, this request is DENIED.

Reason for this decision: The services requested are not medically necessary. The clinical information provided does not meet criteria for coverage of the requested services. Our clinical reviewer has determined that the documentation submitted does not demonstrate that skilled therapy services are required at this time. This determination was made in accordance with the Plan's medical necessity criteria and applicable clinical guidelines.

If you believe this decision is incorrect, you or your authorized representative may file an appeal within sixty (60) calendar days of the date of this notice. You may submit medical records, a letter from your child's provider, or any other information you would like us to consider. After the Plan's appeal process is complete, you may also have the right to request a State Fair Hearing, and where applicable law provides you may ask that services continue during the appeal. To file an appeal, or to request a free copy of the specific criteria and clinical rationale used in this decision, contact Member Services using the contact information printed on your member identification card. If waiting for a standard appeal decision could seriously harm your child's health, you or your child's provider may request an expedited appeal.

Sincerely,
Utilization Management Department
